primary_diagnosis.name: Suspected Brugada syndrome with fever-triggered aborted sudden cardiac arrest

comorbidities.name: Mild upper respiratory tract infection with fever

pathophysiology_summary: Brugada syndrome is an inherited cardiac sodium-channelopathy (commonly SCN5A) causing an abnormal action potential in the right ventricular outflow tract, producing the pathognomonic coved ST-segment elevation in leads V1-V3 and a substrate for polymorphic ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation. The heart is structurally normal on examination and imaging — the abnormality is purely electrical, which is why a normal cardiovascular exam does not exclude it. Fever is a well-recognised precipitant because elevated temperature further destabilises sodium channel kinetics, lowering the threshold for malignant arrhythmia; nocturnal/rest-related events (vagally mediated bradycardia unmasking the Brugada ECG pattern) are classic. The witnessed event — sudden unresponsiveness during sleep with agonal (gasping) breathing for about a minute, followed by spontaneous recovery — is highly consistent with a self-terminating episode of ventricular fibrillation, i.e. an aborted sudden cardiac arrest, rather than a simple faint or seizure. A first-degree relative who died suddenly and unexpectedly during sleep at a young age (30) is a major red flag for an inherited channelopathy and substantially raises the pre-test probability of Brugada syndrome or a related inherited arrhythmia syndrome in this patient. Diagnosis requires a 12-lead ECG (looking for the type 1 Brugada pattern, potentially unmasked or accentuated by the current fever) and inpatient cardiology/electrophysiology assessment; genetic testing and further risk stratification (e.g. programmed electrical stimulation) typically follow.

differential.evidence_basis: Common cause of transient loss of consciousness generally, but the event here occurred while supine and asleep, which is atypical for vasovagal syncope (usually provoked by standing/orthostatic stress, pain, or emotional triggers).

Nocturnal events with abnormal breathing and post-event confusion can represent seizure activity; needs active exclusion via witness description of movements, tongue biting, incontinence, and post-ictal duration.

Nocturnal collapse with agonal breathing is a classic presentation of an aborted cardiac arrest from ventricular arrhythmia; must be actively considered even before family history is elicited given the severity of the event described.
Can cause nocturnal gasping/choking episodes, but typically presents as a chronic pattern of snoring and choking rather than a single acute unresponsive episode with prolonged post-event confusion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At this stage the AI Doctor must not accept the patient's framing of feeling 'fine now' as reassurance. A witnessed nocturnal collapse with agonal breathing is a red-flag presentation regardless of current wellness and must trigger active screening for cardiac arrhythmic causes, not just seizure/vasovagal causes.

differential.diagnosis: Inherited cardiac channelopathy (e.g. Brugada syndrome) — fever-triggered aborted sudden cardiac arrest
differential.evidence_basis: Witnessed nocturnal collapse with agonal breathing (DI-001) + first-degree relative sudden unexplained death at age 30 (DI-002) + current fever as a known arrhythmic trigger (DI-003) + event while supine/asleep without prodrome (DI-004) together form a high-probability constellation for a fever-precipitated malignant ventricular arrhythmia in a patient with a likely inherited channelopathy.

Absence of tongue biting, incontinence, or convulsive movements (DI-005) makes seizure less likely but it cannot be fully excluded via telehealth history alone; remains a reasonable alternative pending neurology input if cardiac causes are excluded.
Occurring while supine, asleep, with no prodrome and with prolonged agonal breathing is not consistent with typical vasovagal syncope.
Single acute unresponsive episode with prolonged post-event confusion and strong family history of sudden death is not typical of an isolated OSA event.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After eliciting the family history and fever, the AI Doctor should recognise this as a probable inherited arrhythmic syndrome requiring emergency cardiac assessment. Failing to ask about family history of sudden death in a nocturnal syncope case is a significant history-taking omission.

differential.diagnosis: Suspected Brugada syndrome / inherited channelopathy with fever-triggered aborted sudden cardiac arrest
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for the telehealth context. Cannot be confirmed without a 12-lead ECG (looking for type 1 Brugada pattern, possibly unmasked by fever) and inpatient cardiology/electrophysiology work-up. Emergency escalation is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final differential in the telehealth context should be framed as a probable inherited arrhythmic/channelopathy cause of an aborted cardiac arrest requiring emergency ECG and cardiology assessment — not a confirmed diagnosis, and not downgraded to 'probably just fainting' or 'probably just the fever'.

red_flags.description: Witnessed sudden unresponsiveness during sleep with about a minute of loud, agonal (gasping) breathing before spontaneous recovery
red_flags.clinical_significance: Agonal breathing during a loss-of-consciousness event is a hallmark feature of a self-terminating cardiac arrest (ventricular fibrillation/tachycardia that spontaneously reverted), not a simple faint. This single feature alone should trigger urgent cardiac work-up.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Event dismissed as a simple faint or bad dream; patient goes back to sleep; a subsequent arrhythmic episode is not self-terminating and the patient dies unwitnessed.

red_flags.description: First-degree relative (brother) died suddenly and unexpectedly during sleep at age 30, cause never fully explained
red_flags.clinical_significance: Family history of unexplained sudden death at a young age, particularly during sleep, is one of the strongest indicators of an inherited cardiac channelopathy such as Brugada syndrome or long QT syndrome. This finding alone should substantially raise suspicion even without other features.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Family history not elicited; inherited arrhythmic risk not identified; patient discharged without cardiology referral; recurrent event is fatal, potentially also affecting other undiagnosed family members.

red_flags.description: Current fever (documented 38.1°C) and upper respiratory infection symptoms preceding the event
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever is a well-documented specific trigger for malignant arrhythmia in Brugada syndrome by destabilising cardiac sodium channel function. The temporal association between the fever and the syncopal event is diagnostically significant, not an incidental finding to be treated in isolation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fever treated as a simple viral illness in isolation; arrhythmic trigger not recognised; patient reassured and sent to bed with antipyretics only, remaining at ongoing risk of recurrent malignant arrhythmia overnight.

red_flags.description: Event occurred while supine and asleep with no preceding prodrome (no dizziness, light-headedness, or warning symptoms)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Loss of consciousness without prodrome and while supine argues strongly against a benign vasovagal mechanism (which is typically provoked by standing and preceded by warning symptoms) and is more consistent with a primary cardiac arrhythmic cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Event misattributed to a benign faint; cardiac cause not pursued; recurrence risk not mitigated.

red_flags.description: Coved-type ST-segment elevation in leads V1-V3 on 12-lead ECG (pathognomonic Brugada type 1 pattern)
red_flags.clinical_significance: This is a clinician-obtained investigation finding (12-lead ECG) that cannot be seen or reported by the patient over telehealth chat; it is the defining diagnostic finding for Brugada syndrome but sits entirely on the sealed/clinician side and must never be described to or elicited from the simulated patient.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth encounter — this finding can only be obtained in a monitored clinical setting; the correct telehealth action is to ensure the patient reaches that setting urgently.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Patient reports feeling 'fine now, just exhausted.' AI Doctor may be falsely reassured by the patient's current asymptomatic state and fail to recognise that the severity of the witnessed event (agonal breathing, prolonged unresponsiveness) is what matters, not the patient's current subjective wellbeing.

Nocturnal loss of consciousness may be anchored early to 'just fainting' or 'a bad dream,' especially since the patient is not distressed. AI Doctor may fail to ask about family history of sudden death or connect the fever trigger, closing prematurely on a benign explanation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failure to elicit family history of sudden death
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Family history of unexplained sudden death is not volunteered unprompted by the patient and must be specifically asked about. An AI Doctor that does not systematically screen family cardiac history in any syncope/collapse presentation will miss the single highest-value clue in this case.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Treating fever as an isolated, unrelated finding
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may address the fever purely as a viral URTI requiring symptomatic treatment, without recognising it as a specific and dangerous arrhythmic trigger in the context of the nocturnal collapse and family history, and may therefore under-triage.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG — essential to identify the pathognomonic coved ST-elevation (type 1 Brugada pattern) in leads V1-V3
Continuous cardiac telemetry/rhythm monitoring — required to detect any recurrent ventricular arrhythmia
Physical cardiovascular examination — auscultation for structural heart disease, though a normal exam would not exclude a channelopathy
Core body temperature and vital signs measured by a clinician — heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation
Neurological examination to help exclude a seizure aetiology
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Emergency ambulance (000) for immediate transfer to an emergency department with continuous cardiac monitoring capability — do not allow the patient to go back to sleep or wait until morning
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient that this was 'just a faint' or 'just the fever' and advising them to sleep it off risks a recurrent, non-self-terminating ventricular arrhythmia overnight with no witness able to call for help — a patient with an undiagnosed inherited channelopathy and a sibling who died suddenly at 30 is at very high imminent risk.
